coredata.dc:title: UV-activated “blue bulbs” for photodecomposition and adsorption of bilirubin: Strategic nanoarchitectonics to remove protein-bound toxins
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yilin
Wei, Ran
Yang, Xijing
Liang, Jiahao
Liu, Xianda
Cheng, Shengjun
Chen, Shifan
Ling, Ziyue
Xiao, Yujie
Xu, Yuanting
Zhao, Weifeng
Zhao, Changsheng
coredata.dc:description: Massive loss of proteins is common in patients on long-term blood purification therapy, there is a strong inverse association between the risk of death and serum albumin concentration. Removing toxins and leaving behind albumin is an arduous task in the field of blood purification. Inspired by the dissociation-transportation process of protein-bound toxins in renal tubule, we propose multi-point photodecomposition accompany with adsorption strategy as a novel treatment modality in hemoperfusion. As a proof of concept, bilirubin is chosen as the research object since it is a typical protein-bound toxin that needs to be transported by strong binding to albumin in blood environment. Interestingly, blue light can efficiently break bilirubin into some highly polar water soluble diazo compounds, the decomposed products of bilirubin are water-soluble and loosely bound to albumin. Herein, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and polymer science are combined in the nanoarchitectonics processes. The “photodecomposition-adsorption” platform is designed by embedding GQDs in poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride-acrylamide) (poly (DDAC-AAm) hydrogel (PDMG) microspheres. In order to reduce the influence of cationic polymer on blood component, hyaluronic acid (HA) are coated on the surface of the PDMG (PDMG@HA) microspheres by electrostatic interaction. Owing to the luminescence property of GQDs, the PDMG@HA microspheres emit blue light under ultraviolet activation and decompose bilirubin into water soluble diazo compounds, just like numerous “blue bulbs”. The water-soluble bilirubin debris could be dissociated from albumin and adsorbed by “blue bulbs”, which greatly reduce the metabolic burden on the body. In vitro biocompatibility results demonstrate that the PDMG@HA microspheres show lower protein adsorption and blood cell toxicity than the PDMG microspheres. The photodecomposition and adsorption amount of bilirubin is 187.8 mg/g in vitro. In hyperbilirubinemia rabbit hemoperfusion, the “blue bulbs” remove 70.9 % total bilirubin (TBIL) from plasma and defer the liver damage of biliary obstruction. Notably, the bilirubin photodecomposition is persisted to the end of the treatment, and some of the bilirubin debris have been adsorbed by the “blue bulbs”. The strategy combining both photodecomposition and adsorption opens a new route for the treatment of blood-related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hyperbilirubinemia
Bilirubin
Adsorbent
Graphene quantum dots
Photodecomposition

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of GQDs and vGQDs
Preparation and characterization of the PDMG@HA microspheres
Biocompatibility of the PDMG@HA microspheres in vitro
Bilirubin adsorption and decomposition in vitro
Therapeutic efficacy and biosafety in hyperbilirubinemia rabbits
Characterization of the GQDs and vGQDs
Evaluation of the biocompatibility of the microspheres in vitro
Therapeutic efficacy and safety in hyperbilirubinemia rabbits

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52122306, U21A2098 and 52003179), Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (2023NSFSC0983), Sichuan University Dedicated Postdoctoral R&D Fund (2023SCU12019), 1.3.5 project (ZYJC21014 and ZYJC21010) for disciplines of excellence West China Hospital at Sichuan University. The graphics in this work were created with the software tool BioRender. The authors would like to thank Yanping Huang for the TEM analysis at the College of Chemistry and Engineering, Sichuan University. The SEM analysis were supported by Chao He at the College of Polymer Science and Engineering, Sichuan University. The pathological technical support was provided by Li Li, Fei Chen, Chunjuan Bao, and Yang Deng from the Institute of Clinical Pathology, West China Hospital, Sichuan University. The animal experiments were supported by Xijing Yang (The Experimental Animal Center of West China Hospital, Sichuan University).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and characterization of GQDs and vGQDs. (A) Schematic diagram for the preparation of GQDs and vGQDs. (B) TEM image of GQDs. (C) Size distribution of GQDs. (D) HRTEM image of GQDs. (E) FTIR spectra for GQDs and vGQDs. (F) 1H NMR spectra for GQDs and vGQDs. (G) PL emission spectra for GQDs (1 mg/mL, the inset is the digital photos of H2O (left) and GQDs (right) under 365 nm UV lamp).

Preparation and characterization of PDMG@HA microspheres. (A) Schematic diagram for the preparation of PDMG@HA microspheres. (B) Optical image of PDM@HA and PDMG@HA microspheres. (C) The SEM images of surface and cross section for PDMG@HA microspheres. (D) The FTIR spectra of PDMG and PDMG@HA microspheres. (E) The compressive stress − strain curve of PDMG@HA microspheres. (F) Reflection spectra and reflection microscopic images of PDMG@HA microspheres under UV on and UV off.

Biocompatibility evaluation of the PDMG and PDMG@HA microspheres in vitro. (A) The adsorption amounts of the microspheres for BSA (n = 3). (B) Hemolysis ratio and digital photos of the microspheres (n = 3). The negative control was normal saline; the positive control was deionized water. (C, D) The generated C3a and C5a in blood after co-culturing with the microspheres for 1 h. The results were measured by ELISAs (n = 3). The negative control was PBS, the positive control was cobra-venom factor (CVF, 25 μg/mL). (E) Blood cell count for pristine blood and blood after incubation with the microspheres (n = 3). The control group was fresh whole blood. WBC: white blood cells, RBC: red blood cells, PLT: platelets, HCT: hematocrit, PCT: plateletcrit. (F) Clotting times of PPP after co-culturing with the microspheres for 30 min (n = 3). PPP: platelet-poor plasma, PT: prothrombin time, APTT: activated partial thromboplastin time, FIB: fibrinogen, TT: thrombin time.

Bilirubin adsorption and decomposition in vitro. (A) Illustration for the process of adsorption and photodegradation by PDMG@HA microspheres. (B) Effect of different amounts of adsorbents on bilirubin removal efficiency (n = 3). Different amounts of adsorbents (wet weight) were added to 10 mL bilirubin solution (T = 37 ℃, initial bilirubin = 200 mg/L, UV = 2 W). (C) Bilirubin adsorption and photodegradation kinetics of the microspheres (T = 37 ℃, initial bilirubin = 200 mg/L, UV = 2 W), (n = 3). (D) The corresponding adsorption and photodegradation kinetics for the microspheres. (E) Adsorption and photodegradation ability of the microspheres at different initial concentrations (T = 37 ℃, t = 3h, UV = 2 W), (n = 3). (F) Adsorption performance of the microspheres in the dark and photodegradation performance of the microspheres in the UV light (T = 37 ℃, initial bilirubin = 200 mg/L, UV = 2 W), (n = 3). (G) Images of the microspheres and bilirubin solution at 0, 180, 360 min during adsorption performance of the microspheres in the dark and photodegradation performance of the microspheres in the UV light. (H) The UV–vis spectra of bilirubin solution contacting with PDMG@HA microspheres in dark and under UV light irradiation.

Bilirubin removal efficiencies of PDMG@HA microspheres under UV and BL irradiation in plasma. (A) The concentrations of TBIL and DBIL in plasma after treated with PDMG@HA microspheres under UV and BL irradiation (n = 3). TBIL: total bilirubin, DBIL: direct bilirubin. (B) The concentrations of TP, ALB and GLB in plasma after treated with PDMG@HA microspheres under UV and BL irradiation (n = 3). TP: total protein, ALB: albumin, GLB: globulin. (C) SEM images of bilirubin and bilirubin debris. (D) Photos of PDMG@HA microspheres under UV irradiation and the radiation distribution simulated by COMSOL Multiphysics. (E) Photos of PDMG@HA microspheres under BL irradiation and the radiation distribution simulated by COMSOL Multiphysics. (F) The integrated radiation intensities of PDMG@HA microspheres under UV and BL irradiation.

Hemoperfusion therapy in hyperbilirubinemia rabbit models. (A) Schematically illustrating hemoperfusion therapy and the circulation direction. (B) Photos of the extracorporeal circulation tubes during hemoperfusion therapy. (C) Photos of the column filled with PDMG@HA microspheres under UV irradiation during hemoperfusion therapy and the blue light distribution in the column simulated by COMSOL Multiphysics. (D) The concentrations of TBIL and DBIL during hemoperfusion therapy (n = 3). TBIL: total bilirubin, DBIL: direct bilirubin. (E) The main indicators of liver function during hemoperfusion therapy (n = 3). ALP: alkaline phosphatase, ALT: alanine aminotransferase, AST: aspartate aminotransferase, GGT: γ-glutamyl transpeptidase. (F) Photos of the column filled with PDMG@HA microspheres before and after treatment. (G) Heatmap of the bilirubin decomposition products and the chemical structure of the bilirubin decomposition products based on the negative, positive and treatment groups (n = 3).

Biosafety of the PDMG@HA microspheres in vivo. (A, B) Routine blood tests during treatment (n = 3). (C-F) Blood biochemistry analysis during treatment (n = 3). WBC: white blood cells, RBC: red blood cell, PLT: platelets, HGB: hemoglobin, HCT: hematocrit, Cl: serum chlorine, K: serum potassium, Na: serum sodium, Ca: serum calcium, CREA: creatinine, UA: uric acid, UREA: urea, TP: total protein, ALB: albumin, GLB: globulin, TG: triglyceride, CHOL: cholesterol, LDL-C: low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, HDL-C: high density lipoprotein. (G) H&E staining images of major organs of negative control, positive control (hyperbilirubinemia rabbits without treatment), and treatment group (hyperbilirubinemia rabbits with treatment) at high magnification. (H) Immunohistochemical staining images of liver tissues (IL-6, TNF-α, TUNEL, Ki-67 and α-SMA), ten regions were selected to calculate the positive ratios by Qupath software.

Serum metabonomics analysis of hyperbilirubinemia rabbits treated by hemoperfusion. (A) Heatmap of the differential metabolites based on the negative, positive and treatment group (n = 3). (B) KEGG pathway enrichment analysis of significantly differential serum metabolites across three groups. Pathway analysis using MetaboAnalyst tool. The sizes of dots represent the number of related metabolites. The colors of dots represent the P value. (C) Network diagram of metabolic pathway and the related metabolites based on the negative, positive and treatment groups. The blue dots represent metabolic pathways; the sizes of blue dots represent the numbers of related metabolites. The red dots represent metabolites. (D, E) Levels of selected dominant metabolites (ATP and L-Arginine) across three groups (n = 3).

head.ce:title: UV-activated “blue bulbs” for photodecomposition and adsorption of bilirubin: Strategic nanoarchitectonics to remove protein-bound toxins

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Polymer Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Polymer Science and Engineering
State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Polymer Science and Engineering, State Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610065, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Animal Experiment Center West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Animal Experiment Center West China Hospital

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Animal Experiment Center West China Hospital, Sichuan University, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Massive loss of proteins is common in patients on long-term blood purification therapy, there is a strong inverse association between the risk of death and serum albumin concentration. Removing toxins and leaving behind albumin is an arduous task in the field of blood purification. Inspired by the dissociation-transportation process of protein-bound toxins in renal tubule, we propose multi-point photodecomposition accompany with adsorption strategy as a novel treatment modality in hemoperfusion. As a proof of concept, bilirubin is chosen as the research object since it is a typical protein-bound toxin that needs to be transported by strong binding to albumin in blood environment. Interestingly, blue light can efficiently break bilirubin into some highly polar water soluble diazo compounds, the decomposed products of bilirubin are water-soluble and loosely bound to albumin. Herein, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and polymer science are combined in the nanoarchitectonics processes. The “photodecomposition-adsorption” platform is designed by embedding GQDs in poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride-acrylamide) (poly (DDAC-AAm) hydrogel (PDMG) microspheres. In order to reduce the influence of cationic polymer on blood component, hyaluronic acid (HA) are coated on the surface of the PDMG (PDMG@HA) microspheres by electrostatic interaction. Owing to the luminescence property of GQDs, the PDMG@HA microspheres emit blue light under ultraviolet activation and decompose bilirubin into water soluble diazo compounds, just like numerous “blue bulbs”. The water-soluble bilirubin debris could be dissociated from albumin and adsorbed by “blue bulbs”, which greatly reduce the metabolic burden on the body. In vitro biocompatibility results demonstrate that the PDMG@HA microspheres show lower protein adsorption and blood cell toxicity than the PDMG microspheres. The photodecomposition and adsorption amount of bilirubin is 187.8 mg/g in vitro. In hyperbilirubinemia rabbit hemoperfusion, the “blue bulbs” remove 70.9 % total bilirubin (TBIL) from plasma and defer the liver damage of biliary obstruction. Notably, the bilirubin photodecomposition is persisted to the end of the treatment, and some of the bilirubin debris have been adsorbed by the “blue bulbs”. The strategy combining both photodecomposition and adsorption opens a new route for the treatment of blood-related diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Blood purification has been widely used nowadays for the treatment of renal failure, liver failure and immune system diseases. [1–3] According to the physicochemical characteristics of blood toxins, they are classified as small water-soluble compounds with low molecular mass (<500 Da), middle molecules (≥500 Da) and protein-bound solutes. Small molecules can be cleared by routine hemodialysis, middle molecules can be cleared by ultra-high flux hemodialysis and hemofiltration. [4] However, protein-bound toxins are difficult to be removed by hemodialysis or hemofiltration since the protein-toxin complexes have large molecular weight.

Protein-bound toxins include bilirubin, indoxyl sulfate, p-cresylsulfate and so on. [5] These toxins are lipid-soluble compounds, the protein binding ratios between toxins and human serum albumin are more than 90 %. [6–8] In clinic, the combination of hemoperfusion and hemodialysis therapy can be used to remove protein-bound toxins. Nevertheless, patients may loss 5–15 g protein and around 2–4 g amino acids after daily peritoneal dialysis. [9,10] Massive loss of albumin may cause malnutrition, edema and immunodeficiency in humans and there is a strong inverse association between the risk of death and serum albumin concentration in patients on long-term blood purification therapy. [11] Removing toxins and leaving behind albumin is an arduous task in the field of blood purification.

In human body, protein-bound toxins are dissociated from albumin and then the unbound toxins are transported from the blood to the intracellular space via cellular membranes, the process is specifically driven by the specific organic anion transporters. [12] Inspired by the dissociation-transportation process of protein-bound toxins in renal tubule, we guess it may be an effective method to make toxins separating from albumin firstly, then adsorb the dissociative toxins by adsorbents. As a proof of concept, bilirubin is chosen as the research object. Bilirubin is a typical protein-bound toxin, it needs to be transported by strong binding to albumin in blood environment since they are lipophilic and hydrophobic. [13,14] Interestingly, the oxidative products of bilirubin are water-soluble and loosely bound to albumin. [15,16] Therefore, the dissociation-adsorption method may be effective in hyperbilirubinemia therapy.

Combined with previous studies on bilirubin, blue light (400–500 nm) can efficiently break bilirubin into some highly polar water soluble diazo compounds that body can get rid of through the urine and stools. [17,18] We proposed a hypothesis that endowing adsorbents with blue light emission performance so that the adsorbents could decompose bilirubin and adsorb the bilirubin debris. Compared to using an external blue light excitation device to decompose bilirubin during hemoperfusion, the adsorbents with blue light emission performance just like numerous “blue bulbs” in the hemoperfusion column, they could realize multi-point photodecomposition of bilirubin at a lower power owing to shorter working distance and larger radiation area. In addition, the water-soluble bilirubin debris could be dissociated from albumin and adsorbed by “blue bulbs”, which can greatly reduce the metabolic burden on the body.

In this study, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are embedded in positively charged hydrogel microspheres owing to their extraordinary optical and electronic properties, [19,20] the hydrogel microspheres can emit blue light under ultraviolet (UV) excitation. [21] Although the GQDs are chemically inert, and some studies suggest the GQDs have no potential toxicity in vivo, [22–24] the leakage of nanoparticles requires serious consideration. To prevent the GQDs from leaking out of the microspheres, vinyl molecules are introduced to GQDs (vGQDs), thus the vGQDs could polymerize with diallyldimethylammonium chloride (DDAC), acrylamide (AAm) to form the GQDs embedded positively charged hydrogel microspheres (PDMG). The GQDs-embedded microspheres greatly improve the stabilities and biosafety of nanomaterials. The PDMG microspheres are prepared by precipitated droplets in-situ cross-linking polymerization, [25] the polymerization for microspheres is completed during 40 s, which has great potential for industrial production. In order to shield the influence of cationic polymer on blood component, hyaluronic acid (HA) is coated on the surface of the PDMG microspheres by electrostatic interaction. The obtained PDMG@HA microspheres are characterized from the structure, luminescence property, hemocompatibility, adsorption and decomposition properties to bilirubin. Furthermore, the safety and efficacy of the PDMG@HA microspheres are verified in hyperbilirubinemia rabbit models, and the bilirubin debris were further detected in plasma.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of GQDs and vGQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing L-glutamic acid (Fig. 1
A). To prevent the GQDs from leaking out of the microspheres, vinyl molecules were introduced to GQDs by the reaction of GMA with the amine groups on the GQDs, thus the vGQDs could polymerize with other monomers. The TEM image of GQDs is shown in Fig. 1 B, the as-prepared GQDs were spherical shape and the diameters of the GQDs were about 3−6 nm (Fig. 1 C). The lattice spacing of GQDs was 0.233 nm from the HRTEM image (Fig. 1 D), which was coordinating to the graphitic carbon. [26] The GQDs and vGQDs were characterized by FTIR spectra (Fig. 1 E), two peaks around 3500∼3300 cm−1 were ascribed to the N–H stretching vibration of primary amine in GQDs, after the reaction with GMA, there was just one peak around 3500∼3300 cm−1, which was ascribed to the N–H stretching vibration of the secondary amines in vGQDs. These results indicated that the primary amine groups in GQDs were reacted with the epoxy groups in GMA. In the NMR spectra for GQDs and vGQDs (Fig. 1 F), the newly appeared 1H NMR peaks at δ 6.08 and δ 5.66 (−CH = CH2) indicated the successful linkage of vinyl onto the GQDs’ surface. [27] The photoluminescence performance of GQDs was size-dependent and shape/edge-state variations. [28] As shown in Fig. 1 G, the GQDs had a typical wavelength-dependent photoluminescence behavior, and the strongest fluorescence emission was around at 440 nm under 360 nm excitation, which suggested that the GQDs emitted blue light under UV excitation. The red-shifted phenomenon of GQDs is corresponding to giant red-edge effect as Scott et al. reported. [29] When GQDs are in a polar solvent and excited by a light source, an additional relaxation step (solvation) occurs in the fluorescence process. After excitation, the fluorophore and the solvent dipole are no longer in equilibrium. The solvent dipole can rotate to align with the excited fluorophore, reducing the interaction energy and lowering the energy of the excited fluorophore. Therefore, the solvation process is usually complete before fluorescence emission, and the net result of solvent relaxation is red-shift. After reaction with GMA, the strongest fluorescence emission of vGQDs shifted to 448 nm (Figure S1), which was also within the blue light range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the PDMG@HA microspheres
ce:section.ce:para:
The preparation procedure of the PDMG@HA microspheres is shown in Fig. 2
A, the precursor solution was dropped into hot silicon oil. The liquid droplets converted into solid microspheres through thermal-initiated in-situ cross-linking polymerization (approximately 40 s). To reduce the influence of cationic polymer on blood component, the PDM (poly (diallyldimethylammonium chloride-acrylamide)) and PDMG microspheres were immersed in 1 % HA solution to shield their positively charged surface. The PDM@HA microspheres were prepared to compare with PDMG@HA microspheres in the photodecomposition experiments. The optical image of the PDM@HA and PDMG@HA microspheres is shown in Fig. 2 B, the PDM@HA microspheres were colorless transparent. When vGQDs were introduced, the color of the PDMG@HA microspheres turned yellow. The surface and cross-sectional images of PDMG@HA microspheres are shown in Fig. 2 C, the PDMG@HA microspheres showed porous and spherical surfaces. In the cross-sectional images, the PDMG@HA microspheres showed abundant macropores. As Figure. S2 and Table S1 shows, the pore size of PDMG@HA microspheres was mainly distributed in 100 ∼ 400 nm and 10 ∼ 40 μm, the porosity of PDMG@HA microspheres was approximately 82.6 %, the pore volume was 4.1 mL/g, the total pore area was 2.7 m2/g. Moreover, the median pore diameter of PDM@HA (81.7 μm) was larger than that of PDMG@HA (20.3 μm). This might be the introduction of vGQDs increased the degree of crosslinking in PDMG@HA microspheres. The FTIR spectra of HA, PDMG and PDMG@HA microspheres are shown in Fig. 2 D, the peaks at 1665 cm−1 were attributed to the CO stretching vibration of acylamino in AAm, both PDMG and PDMG@HA microspheres had acylamino groups. After the introduction of HA, the CO stretching vibration of carboxyl was shifted to 1727 cm−1. The increase in the CO stretching vibration of carboxyl was ascribed to the electron capture effect of quaternary ammonium groups. In addition, the CO stretching vibration and asymmetric stretching vibration of sugar ring were observed at 1080 cm−1 and 1150 cm−1, respectively. These results indicated that HA was successfully introduced in the PDMG@HA microspheres. The loading amount of HA was calculated by measuring the concentration of HA before and after incubating with the PDMG microspheres, as shown in Figure S3, the concentration of HA was decreased from 10 mg/mL to 6.4 mg/mL after incubating with PDMG microspheres, and the calculated adsorption amount was 35.6 mg/g. During clinical hemoperfusion, the blood hydrodynamic pressure in the perfusion column is approximately 500 mmHg. As shown in Fig. 2 E, the strain of PDMG@HA microspheres was 23.6 % at 500 mmHg, which indicated that these microspheres could withstand the blood hydrodynamic pressure. Particularly, the UV-activated blue light emission property was verified by fiber optic spectrometer and reflection microscopic images. As shown in Fig. 2 F, the maximum reflection peak of the PDMG@HA microsphere was around at 440 nm under UV excitation, and the reflection microscopic image was blue. When the UV light was turned off, the PDMG@HA microspheres exhibited no fluorescence emission behavior, and the reflection spectrum was light source spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility of the PDMG@HA microspheres in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
Protein corona is a phenomenon when biomaterials expose to biological fluids, proteins will be rapidly deposited on the surface of biomaterials due to nonspecific or specific adsorption and lead to bio-adverse outcomes. [30,31] Generally, positively charged and hydrophobic materials have strong binding affinity to proteins. [32] Most of the clinical used bilirubin adsorbents are anion exchange resins, which have strong binding affinity to proteins, they might adsorb a good deal of beneficial proteins such as albumin, fibrinogen and coagulation factors. To investigate the protein adsorption amounts of adsorbents, the tightly-bound proteins and loosely-bound proteins was examined using two-step incubation and the concentrations of proteins were measured. As shown in Fig. 3
A, the tightly-bound BSA adsorbed by PDMG and PDMG@HA microspheres were 0.16 and 0.14 mg/g, respectively. The loosely-bound BSA adsorbed by PDMG and PDMG@HA microspheres were 0.77 and 0.39 mg/g, respectively, which has significant difference. In addition, the adsorption amounts of FIB showed the same tendency (Figure S4). These results suggested that the protein adsorption amount of the PDMG@HA microspheres was remarkably decreased compared to that of the PDMG microspheres, which might be owning to the shielding effect of negatively charged and hydrophilic HA. [33] At the end of the hemoperfusion treatment, blood is usually returned with normal saline, the loosely-bound proteins can be washed off with saline to mitigate protein loss.

Red blood cells (RBC) are the most abundant cells in blood, so hemolysis ratio is an important evaluation index to blood-contacting materials. The hemolysis ratio of PDMG microspheres was 5.5 % (Fig. 3 B), which was unqualified for clinical use. The hemolysis ratio of PDMG@HA microspheres was not detected, suggested that no significant RBC rupture or hemoglobin release. In the digital photograph, there was no significant difference between negative control and PDMG@HA microspheres.

Complement system is a recognized pillar of immune surveillance against infection and noxious self-derived antigens. [34,35] Human complement fragment 3a (C3a) and human complement fragment 5a (C5a) are often used as markers to investigate the effect of the materials on complement activation. [36] The generated C3a and C5a of the microspheres had no significant changes compared to pristine blood, confirming no activation of complement system in the collected blood (Fig. 3 C and D).

Blood cell count was also used to evaluate the hemocompatibility of biomaterials. The influence of the microspheres on various blood components is shown in Fig. 3 E, the PDMG microspheres caused the decrease of platelet, suggested that the positively charged biomaterials may cause platelet aggregation or activation. In addition, the number and volume distribution of RBCs, white blood cells (WBCs), and platelets (PLTs) in whole blood did not change significantly after the incubation with the PDMG@HA microspheres.

To investigate the influence on coagulation system of the microspheres, the prothrombin time (PT), activated partial thromboplastin time (APTT), fibrinogen (FIB) and thrombin time (TT) of the plasma after the incubation with the microspheres were measured. As shown in Fig. 3 F, the PT, APTT, FIB and TT of the plasma showed no significant change after co-culturing with the PDMG and PDMG@HA microspheres, which suggested that these microspheres had no significant influence on the coagulation system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bilirubin adsorption and decomposition in vitro
ce:section.ce:para:
Before we systematically carry out the experiment, we tested the influence of UV power on plasma components. As shown in Table S2, high power UV (6 W and 24 W) can decompose bilirubin and other plasma fraction, such as alkaline phosphatase (ALP), aspartate aminotransferase (AST), lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), serum creatine kinase (CK) and serum lipase (LIP). However, the low power UV (2 W) had little effect on plasma fractions, so we chose 2 W UV to carry out the experiment. Although there are many polar groups (2 carbonyls, 4 aminos, 2 carboxyls) in bilirubin molecules, the surface structure of bilirubin has only hydrophobic groups (4 methyls and 2 vinyls groups), since the polar groups are confined inside the bilirubin molecules by intramolecular hydrogen bonds, [37] so bilirubin is hydrophobic in blood environment. Blue light could change the molecular conformation of bilirubin from (ZZ)-bilirubin to (ZE)-bilirubin, (EZ)-bilirubin, and (EE)-bilirubin (Figure S5), [38] the configurational bilirubin photoisomers and decomposition products are more hydrophilic owing to less hydrogen bonds, therefore, they could be easily excreted by bile or urine. [39,40] Meanwhile, these hydrophilic molecules can be easily adsorbed by adsorbents. As shown in Fig. 4
A, the PDMG@HA microspheres emitted blue light under UV activation, the adsorption and photodecomposition process occurred simultaneously. The possible photodegradation products was detected by untargeted liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis (Table S3). The irradiation of bilirubin with blue light leads to biliverdin, dipyrroledialdehyde, and other pyrrolic formation, [15] these water-soluble bilirubin debris could be dissociated from albumin and adsorbed by adsorbents. Firstly, the removal effect of bilirubin by the PDMG@HA microspheres was detected at different weights of adsorbents. As shown in Fig. 4 B, the residual bilirubin concentrations were gradually decreased with increasing the PDMG@HA microsphere amount, accompanied with the color of the bilirubin solution became shallow. The clearance ratio of 100 mg PDMG@HA microspheres was 81.6 % (Figure S6). Based on these results, the related experiments were also performed using 100 mg PDMG@HA microspheres and 10 mL bilirubin solutions.

Time-dependent adsorption-photodecomposition amounts were performed to study the adsorption-photodecomposition kinetics. The PDM@HA microspheres without vGQDs were chosen as the contrast sample to verify the effectiveness of photodecomposition. As shown in Fig. 4 C, the bilirubin removal ratios of PDM@HA and PDMG@HA microspheres were almost the same in the first 30 min under UV irradiation. After 30 min, the bilirubin removal ratios of PDMG@HA microspheres became faster than those of PDM@HA microspheres, suggested that the adsorption-photodecomposition strategy was superior to adsorption. After that, the adsorption of the PDM@HA microspheres gradually reached equilibrium while the PDMG@HA microspheres had a continuous clearance of bilirubin. The clearance ratios of PDM@HA and PDMG@HA microspheres were 60.6 % and 78.2 % at 180 min, respectively. Furthermore, the corresponding kinetic curves fitted well with the pseudo-first-order kinetic model (Fig. 4 D), the kinetic constant (k) of PDMG@HA microspheres was 8.06 × 10-3 min−1, which was 0.7 times higher than that of the PDM@HA microspheres (4.72 × 10-3 min−1).

The adsorption behavior is related to the initial bilirubin concentration. As shown in Fig. 4 E, the adsorption amounts of PDMG@HA microspheres were 36.7, 74.1, 123.1, 132.7, 166.2 and 187.8 mg/g in the 50, 100, 200, 300, 400, and 500 mg/L bilirubin solutions, respectively. The adsorption isotherm of PDMG@HA microspheres fitted well with the Langmuir isotherm (Figure S7), the calculated theoretical maximum adsorption-photodecomposition amount of PDMG@HA microspheres was 191.42 mg/g, which was close to the experimental maximum adsorption-photodecomposition amounts (187.8 mg/g).

In order to further prove that the PDMG@HA microspheres have the ability of photodecomposition, a two-stage successive adsorption-photodecomposition experiment was designed. As shown in Fig. 4 F, the adsorption process was performed in dark environment at the first 3 h and the photodecomposition process was performed in UV environment at the last 3 h. During the first 3 h in dark, the clearance ratios of PDM@HA and PDMG@HA microspheres were 64.1 % and 67.8 %, respectively, which had no significant difference. When triggering to UV irradiation, about 12.3 % of bilirubin were decomposed by PDMG@HA microspheres while 1.8 % of bilirubin were decreased in PDM@HA group, and the color of bilirubin solution in PDMG@HA group became green (Fig. 4 G). Of note, there was a new peak (500 nm) appeared on the UV–vis spectra of bilirubin solution in the PDMG@HA group at the last 3 h, which might be the bilirubin debris (Fig. 4 H). The pore volume of PDMG@HA (4.1 mL/g) was higher than that of PDM@HA microspheres (1.6 mL/g), but they were not an order of magnitude difference (Figure S2 and Table S1). Therefore, the adsorption properties of PDM@HA and PDMG@HA microspheres in dark have no significant difference, and the faster removal by PDMG@HA under UV irradiation (Fig. 4 C) is due to photodecomposition.

Based on the above results of adsorption and photodecomposition, the PDMG@HA microspheres just like “blue blubs”, they indeed emit blue light and promote bilirubin decomposition. The adsorption and photodecomposition process occurred simultaneously, so it was difficult to calculate the respective contributions separately. In order to demonstrate the strategy of UV-activated “blue blub” was superior to the external blue light (BL) excitation device, further tests were performed in plasma.

In order to evaluate the adsorption properties of the microspheres more accurately, the clearances of plasma bilirubin by the PDMG@HA microspheres under UV and BL irradiation for 3 h were tested. As shown in Fig. 5
A, the clearance ratios of total bilirubin (TBIL) and direct bilirubin (DBIL) by the PDMG@HA microspheres under UV irradiation were 62.8 % and 70.8 %, respectively. The clearance ratios of TBIL and DBIL by the PDMG@HA microspheres were 20.9 % and 34.1 % under BL irradiation, respectively. In addition, the concentrations of total protein (TP), albumin (ALB) and globulin (GLB) had no significant difference after UV and BL irradiation (Fig. 5 B), which indicated that the main proteins in plasma were not affected under 2 W UV or BL irradiation. The bilirubin debris were observed by SEM, as Fig. 5 C shows, bilirubin had a distinct elongated nanostructure while the bilirubin debris showed spherical nanostructure, the morphological transition was accordance with another work. [41] The superiority of PDMG@HA microspheres under UV might owing to the larger effective radiation. As shown in Fig. 5 D and E, each PDMG@HA microsphere could emit bright blue light under UV irradiation and the radiation distribution was more uniform than that of BL irradiation. The integrated radiation intensity of PDMG@HA microspheres under UV irradiation was higher than that of BL irradiation (Fig. 5 F).

These results demonstrated that the strategy of UV−activated “blue blubs” was more efficient to the external BL excitation device for hyperbilirubinemia therapy, and it was safe to use low power UV during hemoperfusion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic efficacy and biosafety in hyperbilirubinemia rabbits
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the excellent bilirubin removal ability and good hemocompatibility of the PDMG@HA microspheres, the efficacy and safety of the PDMG@HA microspheres were investigated in vivo using hyperbilirubinemia rabbits. The hyperbilirubinemia rabbits were established by bile duct ligation and the liver function indexes increased greatly after bile duct ligation (Figure S8 A and B), indicated that cholestatic hyperbilirubinemia rabbit model was successfully established.

Actually, ultraviolet blood irradiation (UBI) was extensively used in the 1940s and 1950s to treat many diseases including septicemia, pneumonia, tuberculosis, arthritis, asthma, and even poliomyelitis. [42] Low and mild doses of UV kill microorganisms by damaging the DNA, while any DNA damage in host cells can be rapidly repaired by DNA repair enzymes. [43] Vasil'eva et al. [44] studied varying irradiation levels of UV on both red blood cells and leucocyte–thrombocyte suspensions. The immunosorption activity increased immediately after irradiation in the whole blood and red blood cells; however, the immunosorption capacity in leucocytic–thrombocytic suspensions was lost after 2 days later. To avoid the immune dysregulation of blood cells, the PDMG@HA microspheres were designed to be used in plasma perfusion. The hemoperfusion process was shown in Fig. 6
A and B, there were two main circulation, whole blood circulation and plasma circulation. The blood flow speeds of whole blood circulation and plasma circulation were 5 and 1.2 mL/min, respectively. At first, the hyperbilirubinemia rabbit was anesthetized (Figure S8C), the femoral vein and femoral artery were isolated, punctured and fixed with two-lumen catheters to provide blood access (Figure S8D). Then, whole blood was drawn from the femoral artery and pumped into the plasma separator by Pump 1, the separated plasma was pumped to the quartzose colum filled with PDMG@HA microspheres by Pump 2, the quartzose colum had a UV lamp shade outside, quartz glass has good UV transmittance property so that the PDMG@HA microspheres could absorb the energy of UV light and convert it into blue light (Fig. 6 C) to decompose bilirubin. Finally, the purified plasma was mixed with blood cells in an intravenous drip chamber and infused back into the body from the femoral vein.

The hemoperfusion process lasted for 2 h, the concentrations of TBIL and DBIL are shown in Fig. 6 D. At the first 15 min, the PDMG@HA microspheres adsorbed amounts of bilirubin; then, the adsorption ratios of PDMG@HA microspheres gradually decreased but they exhibited continuous adsorption property, which indicated that the PDMG@HA microspheres still emitted blue light to promote bilirubin decomposition and adsorbed bilirubin debris in the later stage. After 2 h hemoperfusion, the clearance ratios of TBIL and DBIL were 70.9 % and 57.2 %, respectively. Of note, the bilirubin photodecomposition was persisted to the end of the treatment. In addition, the clearance ratios of alkaline phosphatase (ALP), alanine aminotransferase (ALT), aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and γ-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT) were 23.8 %, 25.6 %, 18.3 %, and 30.3 %, respectively (Fig. 6 E). At the end of the treatment, normal saline was used to return blood to the rabbit, and the color of the PDMG@HA microspheres became yellower than that of before treatment (Fig. 6 F).

In vitro bilirubin adsorption and decomposition experiments suggested that, the PDMG@HA microspheres could decompose bilirubin solution to green color and there was a new peak (500 nm) appeared on the UV–vis spectra, which might be the bilirubin debris (Fig. 4 G and H). In depth, the bilirubin decomposition products were further detected in serum by liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). The possible routes of bilirubin photodecomposition in chloroform is shown in Figure S9, which was first reported in 1972. [15] Herein, several decomposition products were detected, including biliverdin, pentdyopents, haematinimide, methylvinylmaleimide and methylmaleimide (Fig. 6 G). Quantitative analysis of bilirubin decomposition products revealed that, the contents of bilirubin, biliverdin and pentdyopents were decreased while the contents of haematinimide, methylvinylmaleimide were increased after the hemoperfusion treatment. These results suggested that the blue light emitting adsorbents indeed decomposed bilirubin and adsorbed some of the decomposition products in plasma.

Blood count assays and blood biochemistry analysis were performed to evaluate the biosafety of the PDMG@HA microspheres. As Fig. 7
A shown, the RBC levels fluctuated steadily during the treatment. The WBC and PLT levels decreased significantly during the first 30 min and then gradually increased to initial levels after 24 h. It is a common phenomenon in clinic that the WBC and PLT levels have a transient decline at the beginning of the hemoperfusion, which might be due to the dilution effect of normal saline or the excessive use of heparin. The levels of hemoglobin (HGB), hematocrit (HCT), electrolytes (Cl, Na, K and Ca), creatinine (CREA), uric acid (UA) and urea (UREA) fluctuated steadily during the treatment (Fig. 7 B-D). The decline of TP, ALB, GLB, triglyceride (TG), cholesterol (CHOL), low-density lipoprotein cholesterol (LDL-C) and high density lipoprotein (HDL-C) at the beginning might be owing to the dilution effect of normal saline, the levels of these indexes returned to the initial levels after 24 h (Fig. 7 E and F).

The rabbits’ main organs were harvested to perform H&E staining. At low magnification (Figure S10), the liver tissue of positive group showed spotty necrosis with severe cytolysis and nuclear fragmentation, which indicated that the liver tissues were severely damaged. At high magnification (Fig. 7 G), the hepatocytes of positive group exhibited hepatic fibrosis, ductular proliferation and inflammatory infiltration. For the treatment group, the liver tissue showed concentric/“onion skin” fibrosis surrounding a bile duct, liver cells exhibited disordered arrangement, degeneration and water fatty degeneration. Notably, the liver tissue of treatment group showed less spotty necrosis than that of the positive group at low magnification (Figure S10). Moreover, the H&E staining of other major organs (heart, spleen, lung, and kidney) showed no apparent lesions among negative, positive and treatment groups. These results demonstrated that the timely hemoperfusion could defer the liver damage of hyperbilirubinemia rabbit, and the PDMG@HA microspheres had good biosafety during hemoperfusion.

Inflammation, apoptosis and fibrosis were further evaluated by immunohistochemical staining in the liver tissues. The extreme exacerbation of systemic inflammation in liver failure is evidenced by the increased levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF, IL-6 and IL-8). [45] Under continuously uncontrolled inflammation and stimulation, apoptosis and pyroptosis of hepatocytes might cause serious liver injury, liver fibrosis, or liver cirrhosis. [46] As shown in Fig. 7 H, the actin ratios of IL-6 and TNF-α cytokines in treatment group were lower than those of the positive group, which indicated that the hemoperfusion treatment could alleviate the hepatocytes damage. Moreover, a large number of TUNEL (+), Ki-67 (+) and α-SMA (+) cells were found in the positive group, indicating that bile duct ligation of rabbit could cause hepatocytes apoptosis and proliferation of fibrous tissue. The treatment group also had inflammation, apoptosis and fibrosis, but the number of TUNEL (+), Ki-67 (+) and α-SMA (+) cells for the treatment group were much less than that of positive group. These results indicated that the hemoperfusion treatment could slow down disease progression by alleviating inflammation, apoptosis and fibrosis.

Liver is a central immunological organ, it plays an essential role in preserving and regulating the levels of amino acid, lipid, glucose in the body, [47–49] therefore, metabolomics research has achieved certain output in the field of liver disease. [50–52] When hepatocytes undergo necrosis, protein, sugar, fat, and other components cannot be broken down or utilized normally, which produces a large number of endogenous metabolic abnormalities. [53] Metabolomics provides theoretical support for efficacy evaluation in the process of artificial liver treatment. To assess the impact of the hemoperfusion treatment on the expression of metabolites and screen the metabolic markers of early liver injury, serum samples were analyzed by metabolomics. As shown in Fig. 8 A, in the serum samples of positive group, the minority of amino acids (N-Acetyl-L-aspartic acid, L-Arginine, Creatinine), fatty acids (20-HETE, 15-Deoxy-d-12,14-PGJ2), and carbohydrates (2-Hydroxyglutarate, Indole-3-acetate, Maltol) were downregulated compared with the negative group, while the majority of fatty acids (Pantothenol, Alpha-Linolenic acid, Colneleic acid, Pimelic acid, Palmitic acid), carboxylic acids (2-Amino-5-oxohexanoate, Succinic acid, 5-Dehydro-4-deoxy-D-glucarate), amino acids (1-Methylhistidine, beta-Alanyl-L-arginine), aromatic amino acid (4-Hydroxystyrene, 4-Hydroxycinnamoylagmatine, 5-Hydroxy-L-tryptophan) and ATP were significantly upregulated. After hemoperfusion, the main differences in substances in the positive group and the treatment group including 3-(2-Hydroxyphenyl) propanoic acid, N-Acetyl-L-aspartic acid, creatinine, 2-Hydroxyglutarate, 4-Hydroxystyrene, biliverdin and so on. These results showed that the serum metabolite of hyperbilirubinemia rabbits was significantly different from that of negative group. There were also significant differences in serum metabolites before and after hemoperfusion therapy in hyperbilirubinemia rabbits.

Combined with Fig. 8
A, metabolic pathways were analyzed through the MetaboAnalyst analysis, and 20 metabolic pathways were identified, which related to the proportion of amino acid metabolism, energy metabolism, lipid metabolism and biosynthesis of other secondary metabolites (Fig. 8 B). Of note, the highest pathway impact was found to be related to bacterial secretion system, which indicated that hyperbilirubinemia rabbits might be infected with bacteria during surgery or biliary tract infection due to bile duct ligation. Within the network of metabolic pathway and the related metabolites, adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and L-Arginine were dominant (Fig. 8 C). The level of ATP in hyperbilirubinemia rabbits was higher than that in healthy rabbits (Fig. 8 D). The increase in ATP suggested that the liver adopted a series of strenuous responses in the early stage of bile duct ligation to cope with the acute attack, such as lipolysis for energy supply and sugar metabolism. L-Arginine is an intermediate product of urea cycle, arginase cleaves L-Arginine to form L-Ornithine while highly toxic ammonia converts to urea for excretion. [54] In this work, the level of L-Arginine in hyperbilirubinemia rabbits was lower than that in healthy rabbits (Fig. 8 E). The decrease of L-Arginine may be related to the massive necrosis of hepatocytes and the decrease of enzymes activity in urea cycle, leading to urea cycle disorders and weakened ammonia metabolism. Nevertheless, there is no high-quality evidence to support the existence of urea cycle disorder in patients with biliary obstruction, and the results still need to be further verified.

Taken together, the PDMG@HA microspheres exhibited excellent bilirubin removal property and satisfied biosafety in hyperbilirubinemia rabbits’ hemoperfusion. Importantly, the idea of the “blue bulbs” that photodecompose and adsorb bilirubin in plasma has been confirmed to be feasible. Metabolomics analysis of serum suggested that liver injury could cause the dysregulation of amino acids metabolism, lipid metabolism and energy metabolism. Moreover, there were high risks of bacterial infection for patients with liver injury, patients who develop liver injury complicated with bacterial infection have high death rates and are frequently unable to clear the infection. [55].

Overall, this is the first trial to develop a kind of adsorbents with both photodecomposition and adsorption properties which can be used in blood environment. The newly designed “blue bulbs” overcome the drawbacks of nanomaterials and phototherapy, thus realizing efficient and safe treatment for hyperbilirubinemia. In this work, the PDMG@HA microspheres possess a hydrophilic surface so they show excellent biocompatibility, they have low affinity to proteins, low hemolysis ratios, no activation of the complement system and coagulation system. In addition, the blue light emission property endows them with persistent ability to decompose bilirubin. Compared to external BL excitation device, the “blue bulbs” have larger effective radiation and it is safe to use low power UV during hemoperfusion. In hyperbilirubinemia rabbit hemoperfusion, the “blue bulbs” remove 70.9 % total bilirubin (TBIL) from plasma and defer the liver damage of biliary obstruction. Notably, the bilirubin photodecomposition persists to the end of the treatment. Although the adsorption equilibrium has reached as early as 15 min after the initial treatment, the “blue bulbs” have huge adsorption capacity and persistent photodecomposition ability until the end of treatment. Encouragingly, this idea could be applied in the removal of other blood toxins, thus promoting the development of advanced blood adsorption materials and realizing wearable artificial liver/kidney in the future.

Despite extensive efforts on developing new adsorbents over the past decades, few of them can be used in clinic. Most of the improvements start from increasing the porosity of adsorbents. Advanced porous materials are beneficial for the adsorption of bilirubin. As reported, cationic porous aromatic frameworks (PAFs) has an adsorption capacity of 1249 mg/g to bilirubin, [56] Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets can adsorb bilirubin up to 1192.9 mg/g, [57] metal organic framework (MOF) materials PCN-333 and MOF-808 exhibit high bilirubin adsorption capacities of 1003.8 and 854.7 mg/g, respectively. [58] Although these porous materials have ultrahigh specific surface area and excellent bilirubin adsorption capacity, their biological and blood safety need further consideration. Besides, the high cost, low production rates and complex preparation method also hinder their commercialization. In stark contrast, we design the adsorbents based on the characteristics of toxin. Bilirubin is light-sensitive, thus we endow the adsorbents with blue light emission property to decompose and adsorb bilirubin. The blue light emission property is realized by GQDs, the GQDs are chemically linked to the polymer substrate so they have great stability and biosafety. The adsorbents are designed to be hydrophilic so that they can adsorb water-soluble bilirubin debris but repel protein adhesion, therefore, the adsorbents achieve competitive adsorption with albumin and reduce the loss of protein during hemoperfusion. Moreover, considering the cost, preparation method and biosafety, this work is accordance with the requirements of the clinical needs and stable industrialized production, which is expected to be commercialized.

Serum metabolomics are used to investigate the photodecomposition products of bilirubin and the treatment efficacies of hemoperfusion in hyperbilirubinemia rabbit. There are significant differences in serum metabolites levels across the negative, positive and treatment groups. Within the network of metabolic pathway and the related metabolites, ATP and L-Arginine are dominant, which indicate that the liver adopted a series of strenuous responses to cope with the acute attack and there might be urea cycle disorders. Although the urea cycle disorder has not been verified, L-Arginine might become a new diagnostic or therapeutic target in early liver injury. Despite these advances, our research still has some problems to be investigated in the future. (1) The metabolic pathway of bilirubin debris has not been well defined, although relevant research suggest that the decomposition products of bilirubin are excreted through bile and urine. [18] (2) As an important medium for communication throughout the body, the changes of blood reflect the final results of systemic metabolism. However, most of the metabolites are concentrated in liver, and only a small amount of them can be released into blood, resulting in that the changes of metabolites in blood are not as obvious as those in liver tissues. Even better, investigating the changes of metabolites in liver tissues, which can reflect the disease status and the therapeutic effect of hemoperfusion more accurately. (3) The metabolites vary considerably in different liver diseases, most of the metabolites detected in this study were lack specificity, which cannot provide specific markers for early disease diagnosis. Perhaps we need a larger sample size to screen for differential metabolites with specificity.

According to the results of this work, we think that the strategy of decomposition and adsorption will be an important direction for the development of hemoperfusion adsorbent. For example, small molecule uremic toxins are difficult to be removed by adsorbent such as urea and protein-bound uremic toxins (PBUTs). If they could be decomposed and then adsorbed by adsorbents, the development of wearable artificial kidney will be greatly promoted. Nevertheless, there are some points to be mentioned. (1) In vivo toxicity of the decomposition products. (2) Metabolic pathways of the decomposition products. (3) Whether the decomposition process has a negative effect on blood components or organs. In the development of new adsorbents in the future, we should change the traditional perspectives and pay more attention to the characteristics of toxins, pathogenic mechanisms and metabolic pathways.

Acrylamide (AAm, 99 %), diallyldimethylammonium chloride (DDAC, 60 % in water), N, N’-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA, 98 %), ammonium persulfate (APS, 98 %), L(+)-glutamic acid (99 %), bilirubin, glycidyl methacrylate (GMA, 97 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), bovine serum albumin (BSA), silicone oil (viscosity 100 ± 8 mPa.s) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. Hyaluronic acid (HA, Mn = 100000) was purchased from Heowns Biochemical Technology Co., LTD. Bovine fibrinogen (FIB, 50–70 % protein) was purchased from Yeasen Biotechnology Co., Ltd. N-hexane (99 %) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Inc., China. Deionized water (DI water) was used throughout the study.

The GQDs were synthesized by pyrolyzing L-glutamic acid as reported. [26] In brief, 2 g L-glutamic acid was melted under 220 ℃, the GQDs were formed when the boiling colorless liquid turned to brown. The product was dispersed in water and stored in 4 ℃. The vGQDs were prepared by the reaction of GMA with amine groups on the GQDs. Briefly, 10 mL of GQD solution and 20 mL of GMA were added to a flask. The mixture was reacted at 30 °C for 24 h. Next, the oil phase of the mixture was discarded, and the water phase was washed with n-hexane to remove the unreacted GMA. The vGQDs solution were stored in 4 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the GQDs and vGQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical structures of the GQDs and vGQDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50, America) using KBr pellets. 1H nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra of the GQDs and vGQDs in D2O solution were acquired on a nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrometer (AV III HD 400 MHz, Bruker, Germany). The fluorescence spectra of the GQDs and vGQDs were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FluoroMax-4, Horiba, Japan). The morphologies of the GQDs and vGQDs were observed by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-F200, JEOL, Japan). The size distribution of the GQDs was measured and plotted using Image J software. The size distributions and pore diameter statistics summary of PDM@HA and PDMG@HA were measured by automatic mercury porosimeter (AutoPore IV 9500, Micromeritic, USA).

The PDMG microspheres were prepared by precipitated droplets in-situ cross-linking polymerization as reported. [25] The precursor solution was feeding from a 5 mL syringe with a 23 G blunt-end needle at 3.6 kV high voltage, and the solution feeding rate was 0.5 mL min−1, then the precursor solution was dropped into a sedimentation column filled with 95 °C silicon oil. The liquid droplets converted into solid microspheres through thermal-initiated in-situ cross-linking polymerization (approximately 40 s). The precursor solution of PDMG microspheres containing 2.5 g AAM, 1.5 g DDAC, 0.42 g MBA, 0.25 g vGQDs and 2.58 g H2O. The PDM microspheres were also prepared as previously mentioned but without vGQDs.

To fabricate the PDMG@HA microspheres, the PDM and PDMG microspheres were immersed into the 1 wt% HA solution. Owing to the electrostatic interaction, HA can shield positively charged surface of the PDMG microspheres, and the resulted microspheres were named as PDM@HA and PDMG@HA, respectively.

The microstructures of the PDMG@HA microspheres were observed by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Japan Phenom Pure, Netherlands). The mechanical properties of the PDMG@HA microspheres were investigated by a universal testing machine (SANS CMT4000, MTS). The fluorescence spectra of the PDMG@HA microspheres under ultraviolet excitation were measured using a fiber optic spectrometer (HR2000+, Ocean optics), and the photos of PDMG@HA microspheres under ultraviolet excitation were acquired by stereoscopic microscope (SteREODiscovery.V12, ZEISS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the biocompatibility of the microspheres in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the biocompatibility of the PDMG and PDMG@HA microspheres, hemolysis ratio, complement activation, contact activation, blood cell count, clotting times, and protein adsorption were evaluated. The experimental details were recorded in Supporting Information.

The adsorption and decomposition kinetics and adsorption isotherms of the microspheres were tested in PBS. The adsorption performance of the microspheres in plasma and the influence of UV or blue light (BL) to plasma were also evaluated. The experimental details were recorded in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Therapeutic efficacy and safety in hyperbilirubinemia rabbits

ce:section.ce:para: The rabbits were given 3 % pentobarbital sodium (1 mL kg−1) before operation, blood was collected and the blood biochemistry was tested. Under laparotomy, the bile duct was ligated with a double ligation, and the abdomen was closed. Rabbits were given intravenous bilirubin (25 mg/kg) the day after bile duct ligation and performed with plasma perfusion.

The scheme of plasma perfusion therapy is shown in Fig. 6 A, two peristaltic pump (KXP100, Kamoer), plasma separator (PlasmafloTM OP-02 W, AsahiKASEI), extracorporeal circulation blood tubes (Connect with silicone hose, joint and Luer taper) and quartzes adsorption column (Filled with 8 mL PDMG@HA microspheres) were used. The rabbit was weighed and anesthetized, the femoral vein and artery were isolated, punctured and fixed with detaining needles. Heparin was used as an anticoagulant and the initial loading dose of 500 IU was followed by a continuous infusion of 200 IU for every 30 min. The blood flow rates were 5 mL/min and 1.2 mL/min for blood pump and plasma pump, respectively. After the hemoperfusion treatment, the operative wound of the rabbit was carefully sutured. Blood biochemistry, blood cell count, and histopathological damage evaluation were carried out 24 h after the extracorporeal hemoperfusion.

Yilin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ran Wei: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xijing Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Jiahao Liang: Validation, Investigation. Xianda Liu: Investigation. Shengjun Cheng: Investigation, Conceptualization. Shifan Chen: Investigation. Ziyue Ling: Validation. Yujie Xiao: Validation. Yuanting Xu: Funding acquisition. Weifeng Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Changsheng Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 52122306, U21A2098 and 52003179), Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (2023NSFSC0983), Sichuan University Dedicated Postdoctoral R&D Fund (2023SCU12019), 1.3.5 project (ZYJC21014 and ZYJC21010) for disciplines of excellence West China Hospital at Sichuan University. The graphics in this work were created with the software tool BioRender. The authors would like to thank Yanping Huang for the TEM analysis at the College of Chemistry and Engineering, Sichuan University. The SEM analysis were supported by Chao He at the College of Polymer Science and Engineering, Sichuan University. The pathological technical support was provided by Li Li, Fei Chen, Chunjuan Bao, and Yang Deng from the Institute of Clinical Pathology, West China Hospital, Sichuan University. The animal experiments were supported by Xijing Yang (The Experimental Animal Center of West China Hospital, Sichuan University).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mattod.2024.09.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

Ding, H. M., and Ma, Y. Q., Biomaterials (2014) 35 (30), 8703
Attwood, S. J., et al., J. Mater. Chem. B (2019) 7 (14), 2349
West, E. E., and Kemper, C., Nat. Rev. Nephrol. (2023) 19 (7), 426
Song, X., et al., Nat. Biomed. Eng. (2021) 5 (10), 1143
